However, at the same time, this Court cannot turn a blind eye to the equally settled principle that the enforcement of rights cannot be divorced from the observance of lawful conduct by those who invoke them, nor can law enforcement agencies be clothed with an unfettered or unregulated mandate that may, in effect, dilute constitutional protections guaranteed to citizens.
v. It is a settled proposition that the relationship between the citizen and the State is neither adversarial in perpetuity nor unidirectional in obligation, rather, it is founded upon a delicate constitutional balance where rights and duties operate in tandem.
vi. Both citizens and State functionaries are, therefore, bound by reciprocal obligations flowing from the same constitutional fabric. It is for this reason that this Court must emphasize that a careful demarcation of jurisdiction, authority, and responsibility is indispensable.

case: C.R No. 71-A of 2013 Syed Muzammal Shah & others Vs Gohar Aman & others
remarks:
West Pakistan Land Revenue Act, 1967 (XVII of 1967), Ss. 40, 53 & 172, West Pakistan General Clauses Act, 1956, S.2(41), Civil Court jurisdiction, special revision of record of rights, validity of notification, mala fide in law, colourable exercise of statutory authority. Civil Court jurisdiction despite statutory bar – Section 172 of the Act bars the jurisdiction of Civil Courts only in respect of matters which the revenue authorities are empowered by the Act to determine. The provision was never intended to place beyond judicial scrutiny every action purportedly taken under the Act. Even where a statutory bar exists, Civil Courts continue to exercise jurisdiction where the impugned action is alleged to be without jurisdiction, mala fide, coram non judice, or in excess of powers conferred by statute. Where mala fide, excess of powers, transgression of jurisdiction, colourable exercise of authority or ultra vires action is alleged, the Civil Court remains the Court of ultimate jurisdiction competent to examine the validity of the impugned action. Sections 53 and 172, read together, preserve the right of a person aggrieved by entries in the record of rights to institute a suit for declaration. (Paras 11, 12, 15 & 18)
Section 40 – Scope and limits of special revision – Section 40 confers an extraordinary supervisory jurisdiction intended to ensure accuracy and reliability of revenue records. It was never designed as a mechanism for reopening settled rights, nullifying judicial findings, or altering proprietary interests through executive fiat. A record of rights records existing rights; it does not create them. Revision of a record of rights is intended to ensure correctness of entries; it is not a substitute for adjudication of disputed title. The validity of an exercise under Section 40 depends upon the justification found in each case and remains subject to judicial scrutiny. (Paras 21, 22 & 23)
Notification under Section 40 – No recommendation by revenue authorities – No reasons recorded – Jurisdictional defect – The local revenue authorities did not recommend special revision. The Deputy Commissioner did not recommend special revision. The Board of Revenue itself initially doubted applicability of Section 40. Yet, notwithstanding these reservations, the impugned notification came to be issued. The notification itself furnished little assistance in explaining why the extraordinary jurisdiction under Section 40 was invoked. No reason whatsoever was disclosed. The notification neither identified any defect in the existing record nor referred to any fraud, mistake, irregularity, inquiry report, complaint, misrepresentation or other circumstance necessitating invocation of such extraordinary power. The notification recorded the conclusion but not the reasoning. (Paras 27, 28, 32 & 34)
Corrigendum – Expansion from village to estate – Effect – A notification under Section 40 contemplates special revision of the record of rights of a revenue estate and not of particular persons, selected khasra numbers or isolated parcels of land. The effect of the corrigendum was not merely textual but substantive, as it sought to bring the notification within the statutory framework of Section 40. Even after issuance of the corrigendum, the exercise did not culminate in a general revision of the record of rights of the entire estate. Instead, the consequential changes remained confined to specific entries affecting the rights of the petitioners. The corrigendum reinforced the impression that the process ultimately adopted was neither transparent nor consistent with the object, scope and requirements of Section 40 of the Act. (Paras 37 & 38)
Revenue record – Longstanding entries – Presumption of correctness – Longstanding entries incorporated in successive Jamabandis carry a statutory presumption of correctness. Such entries are not lightly disturbed. Revenue entries may not be conclusive proof of title, yet longstanding entries repeatedly carried forward in successive settlements and Jamabandis cannot be brushed aside without compelling justification. It is equally well settled that the statutory presumption attaches to entries in the columns of ownership and possession and not to entries in the columns of Lagan or Kaifiyat. (Paras 41, 42, 43, 45 & 46)
Administrative action vis-à-vis prior judicial determinations – Rights and entries that have survived judicial scrutiny over several tiers of adjudication acquire a degree of finality which cannot lightly be disregarded by executive action. While the executive may exercise powers conferred by statute, it cannot, under the guise of administrative action, nullify or circumvent findings rendered by competent Courts. What could not be achieved through litigation cannot subsequently be achieved through administrative action. (Paras 49, 50, 51 & 52)
Notification – Publication in Official Gazette – Effect of non-publication – Evolution of jurisprudence – Section 2(41) of the West Pakistan General Clauses Act, 1956 expressly links the very concept of a notification with publication in the Official Gazette. The legal position emerging from the authorities is that publication in the Official Gazette is not invariably mandatory for every governmental act. However, where the governing statute contemplates publication, or where the notification is intended to affect, alter, diminish or determine the rights and liabilities of citizens, publication ceases to be a matter of form and becomes a substantive requirement. In such cases, publication serves the fundamental purposes of legality, transparency, notice and due process. (Paras 59, 60, 64 & 68)
Notification affecting proprietary and possessory rights – Publication mandatory – The impugned notification was intended to alter entries affecting proprietary, possessory and tenancy rights recorded in the revenue record. The case satisfied both conditions recognised by the Supreme Court: first, the statutory definition expressly contemplated publication in the Official Gazette; secondly, the rights and liabilities of citizens were directly and substantially affected. In these circumstances, publication was not a procedural formality. It was an essential prerequisite to the legal efficacy and enforceability of the notification. A document which has not been published in the Official Gazette, despite being intended to alter vested proprietary and possessory rights, cannot readily be accorded the status of a legally operative notification capable of sustaining the drastic consequences that followed. (Paras 70, 71, 72 & 73)
Section 40 – Cumulative defects – Mala fide in law – Colourable exercise of statutory authority – The local revenue authorities did not recommend special revision; the Deputy Commissioner did not recommend special revision; the Board of Revenue itself initially doubted applicability of Section 40; no reasons were recorded in the notification; the scope of the exercise was subsequently enlarged through a corrigendum; longstanding entries reflected in successive Jamabandis were sought to be disturbed notwithstanding previous litigation concerning the same subject matter; the affected persons were not afforded any meaningful opportunity of hearing; and the notification was never published in the Official Gazette. Viewed collectively, these circumstances left little room for doubt that the impugned notification and the consequential exercise undertaken pursuant thereto travelled beyond the lawful scope of Section 40 and could not be sustained in the eye of law. The cumulative circumstances raised a compelling inference of mala fide in law. The power conferred by Section 40 was employed for a purpose not contemplated by the statute itself and constituted a colourable exercise of statutory authority. (Paras 74, 75 & 76)

case: WP No. 124-A of 2025 Riasat Zaman Vs Govt of Pakistan etc
remarks:
Constitution of Pakistan, 1973 — Article 199 — Autonomous statutory body — National Book Foundation — Statutory Service Rules — Finance Division Office Memorandums — Whether executive instructions can override statutory rules.
Where National Book Foundation was a statutory autonomous body established under an Act of Parliament and its Service Rules were framed by the Board of Governors under section 8 of the Act, executive Office Memorandums issued by Finance Division could not override, amend or nullify the governing statutory framework in absence of lawful adoption by the competent statutory authority. (Paras. 11, 12, 19, 20, 21, 24 and 32)
Service Law — Gratuity — Statutory right — Withdrawal through executive instructions — Legality.
Chapter-IX of National Book Foundation Service Rules, 1973 expressly provided that all confirmed employees would be entitled to contributory provident fund and gratuity, while subsequent Board decision introducing pension scheme preserved option of employees “to continue the existing CP Fund plus gratuity.” Held, such accrued retirement benefits could not legally be defeated through mere executive interpretation unsupported by lawful amendment in governing rules. (Paras. 13, 14, 15, 19, 36 and 37)
Executive instructions — Office Memorandums — Scope and legal effect — Autonomous corporations.
“Executive instructions, office memorandums or administrative notifications cannot override, amend or nullify statutory rules or rights flowing therefrom. Where an autonomous statutory body operates under its own parent statute and service rules framed thereunder, the same prevail over inconsistent executive directions unless lawfully adopted by the competent statutory authority.” (Para. 21)
Finance Division Office Memorandums — Applicability to autonomous statutory bodies — Distinction between Government servants and employees of autonomous corporations.
“The distinction between Government servants and employees of autonomous statutory bodies is legally significant and cannot be ignored.” Office Memorandums issued by Finance Division “for regulation of Federal Government employees do not automatically extend to employees of autonomous corporations governed by their own statutory framework.” (Paras. 30 and 31)
Autonomous statutory bodies — Board of Governors — Competence to regulate service structure.
“The statutory scheme governing the Foundation clearly envisages that policy decisions affecting service structure and retirement benefits must emanate from the competent forum constituted under the Act. Any action taken by subordinate functionaries in derogation of powers vested in the Board of Governors cannot be accorded legal sanctity.” (Para. 39)
Service Rules — Amendment — Requirement of lawful adoption.
Where respondents failed to demonstrate that Board of Governors ever lawfully amended Chapter-IX of the Rules or consciously adopted Finance Division Office Memorandums, gratuity could not be withheld merely on basis of executive instructions. (Paras. 19, 24 and 38)
Retirement benefits — Constitutional protection — Gratuity not a bounty.
“It is settled law that vested and accrued retirement benefits cannot be withdrawn arbitrarily through executive instructions particularly where employees devoted their entire service careers under an existing statutory regime. Gratuity is not a matter of bounty or benevolence but a valuable service right earned through long and continuous service.” (Para. 36)
Retirement benefits — Articles 9 and 14 of Constitution — Scope.
Though controversy related to gratuity rather than pension, underlying constitutional principle remained identical because “retirement benefits earned over decades of service cannot be defeated through mere executive interpretation unsupported by lawful amendment in the governing statutory framework.” (Para. 37)
Autonomous corporations — Financial autonomy — Effect.
“Once the Foundation independently manages its financial affairs and operates under its own service rules, automatic application of Finance Division instructions meant for Government servants cannot be presumed in absence of lawful adoption by the competent authority of the Foundation.” (Para. 32)
Articles 4 & 25 — Equality and fair treatment — Selective denial of gratuity.
Where similarly placed employees and even Managing Directors had been granted gratuity while employees serving for decades were denied same benefit, selective application of executive instructions offended Article 25 of Constitution and violated mandate of fair treatment contemplated under Article 4 thereof. (Para. 33)
Financial constraints — Whether valid ground to deny accrued retirement benefits.
“Financial inconvenience by itself cannot furnish lawful justification for withholding accrued retirement benefits in absence of valid amendment in the rules.” (Para. 41)
Administrative interpretation — Whether can supersede statutory framework.
“Absence of any such material strengthens the petitioner’s contention that the impugned deprivation is based merely upon administrative interpretation rather than any valid amendment in the governing rules.” (Para. 35)

case: W.P No. 5552-P of 2025 Muhammad Usman Vs Cantonment Board through its Executive Officer, Peshawar Cantt & others
remarks:
Cantonments Act, 1924-- Section 280(2)(c) --Pakistan Cantonment Servants Rules, 1954 – temporary appointment- Held- the petitioner being not appointed against a regular sanctioned post through a regular recruitment process. The appointment letter as well as subsequent extension orders appended with the petition clearly demonstrate that the petitioner’s engagement was purely temporary and contractual/daily wage in nature. The terms and conditions embodied therein specifically provided that his services were liable to termination at any time without notice and without assigning any reason. Such stipulation leaves little room for creation of any enforceable vested right in favour of the petitioner. The law is now fairly settled that employees engaged on daily wages, contingent basis, work-charge basis or temporary arrangements do not acquire indefeasible right either to continuation in service or to regularization merely on the basis of length of service.
Pakistan Cantonment Servants Rules, 1954 – Regularization of service- not a vested right- Held-- the petitioner remained engaged throughout on a purely temporary basis as a daily-wage employee receiving fixed monthly wages. Such an arrangement, by its very nature, does not confer any vested or enforceable right to seek regular appointment or continuation in service. It is also pertinent to observe that the relevant statutory framework excludes from the ambit of regularization those employees who are appointed against project posts, work-charge establishments, or are paid from contingencies. The legislative intent behind such exclusion is manifest and unambiguous, that the benefit of regularization is confined to employees appointed on adhoc or contractual basis under the prescribed scheme, and not to those engaged on daily-wages or work-charge arrangements, which are inherently temporary, non-pensionable, and contingent upon the continuation of specific projects, schemes, or availability of funds.
Pakistan Cantonment Servants Rules, 1954 – Extension of service- scope of -- the petitioner was engaged under temporary and time-bound arrangements, which were extended from time to time subject to administrative necessity, availability of posts, and financial resources. However, such extensions cannot, by any stretch of interpretation, convert a temporary engagement into permanent employment. The respondents have specifically pleaded that the services of the petitioner, along with other similarly situated employees, were discontinued on account of financial constraints, HR rationalization, and non-availability of sanctioned posts. In the absence of any statutory protection or vested right in favour of the petitioner, the discontinuation of his temporary engagement cannot be termed unlawful.
Pakistan Cantonment Servants Rules, 1954 – Proper forum for determination of questions – application of-- administrative authorities are the proper forum to determine matters relating to organizational requirements, staffing patterns, and financial sustainability, keeping in view the institutional needs and available fiscal resources. In exercise of constitutional jurisdiction, this Court cannot sit as an appellate authority over administrative decisions nor substitute its own wisdom action is shown to be tainted with mala fide, arbitrariness for that of the competent authorities unless the impugned, discrimination, or patent illegality. It is further well-settled that the question of regularization squarely falls within the domain of policy-making and remains an exclusive prerogative of the Executive. Such matters ordinarily do not invite judicial interference, particularly where no statutory scheme or declared policy conferring a right of regularization has been brought on record.
Ref: Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa v. Sher Aman (2022 SCMR 406); Province of Punjab v. Ahmed Hussain (2013 SCMR 1547); Faraz Ahmed v. Federation of Pakistan through Secretary, Ministry of Communication, Government of Pakistan, Islamabad (2022 PLC 198);
Petitions dismissed.

case: Cr.A No. 27-D of 2024 Dr. Hafiz Muhammad Tahir Vs Muhammad Salam
remarks:
This Court has already recognized and affirmed the jurisdiction of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission in matters relating to medical negligence. In Lady Doctor Nargis v. The State in the following terms:
21. Hence, the law is clear:
(i) no FIR can be registered against a healthcare service provider or a healthcare institution on the allegations of malpractice or negligence except at the instance of the commission;
(ii) any person aggrieved of any malpractice or negligence by a healthcare service provider or institution may file a complaint u/s 13 of ‘the Act of 2015’ before the KP Healthcare Commission;
(iii) it is the duty and responsibility of the commission to expeditiously and diligently pursue and take the complaint to its legal and logical conclusion, which may also, after due process, include lodging of FIR under the relevant provisions of PPC (including section 322);
(iv) it therefore follows that any such FIR registered directly without first approaching the Healthcare Commission would be without lawful authority and liable to be quashed.
Although the said case pertained to initiation of criminal proceedings, this Court clearly held that in matters involving medical malpractice or negligence, the aggrieved person must first approach the HCC by filing a complaint under section 13 of the HCC Act. In Dr. Samdana Wahab v. The State, this Court held that allegations of medical negligence require expert and technical scrutiny and cannot be directly subjected to ordinary criminal process or inquiry by a Judicial Magistrate on the direction of an Ex-Officio Justice of Peace. The HCC is the specialized statutory forum for such matters, and the complainant must first approach it. Consequently, the order directing magisterial inquiry was set aside, while the HCC was directed to entertain the complaint afresh on merits despite limitation. This Court, in Professor Dr. NiamatUllah v. Izaz Khan (PLD 2026 Peshawar 49), has categorically held that the Consumer Protection Court has no jurisdiction to determine matters of medical negligence or malpractice, as such questions require expert assessment by the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission under section 13 of the HCC Act.
The relevant portion is reproduced below:
7. Turning to another aspect of the case whether the Consumer Protection Court is the appropriate forum to determine whether medical malpractice or negligence has occurred, it is well accepted that for a claim for medical/clinical negligence to be established, a medical practitioner has to be found to have breached a duty of care to a patient, who in turn suffers injury as a result of that breach. Demonstrating that a doctor has breached the duty of care is the first major hurdle in any negligence case but this is not always clear cut. There is certainly scope for genuine differences of opinion when it comes to diagnosis and treatment. The complainant\'s contention is that the Consumer Protection Court lacks the jurisdiction and it is the Commission that is to make an assessment in that regard in view of the standards envisaged under section 13 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission Act,2015. Needless to say, liability claims for defective services, as envisaged under Section 13 of the Act ibid, would similarly entail a breach of a duty to be determined by the commission under section 13, it reads:
13. Complaints.---(1) An aggrieved person may, within sixty days from the date of knowledge of the cause of action, file a complaint against a healthcare service provider or healthcare establishment by submitting an application in writing supported by an affidavit, national identity card number and address of the aggrieved person. (2) The Commission shall not entertain an anonymous or pseudonymous complaint against a private health care service provider or healthcare establishment. (3) The Commission shall investigate in a transparent manner, the complaints relating to quality of health services, health services or system and medical negligence. (4) The Commission shall prescribe the procedure for the conduct of investigation to be carried out by the Commission under this Act.
Reliance in this respect is placed on PLD 2019 Lahore 429 Dr. Riaz Qadeer Khan vs Presiding Officer, District Consumer Court, Sargodha and others, the relevant is:
For what has been discussed above, we are persuaded to hold that Healthcare Commission is the only competent forum to investigate into the allegations of maladministration or malpractice by a health service provider and the Consumer Court has no jurisdiction to adjudicate upon such matters. Resultantly, all these writ petitions are allowed and the complaints pending before the learned Consumer Court are directed to be returned for its presentation before the appropriate forum.
8. In view of the above provision of law, the respondent No.l/complainant has not filed any complaint rather approached the Consumer Protection Court, which has no jurisdiction to try such like complaints.
The ratio of the aforesaid judgment directly governs the present controversy; consequently, the complaint ought to have been presented before the HCC, being the competent statutory forum.The Honorable Lahore High Court, in Dr. Riaz Qadeer Khan v. Presiding Officer, District Consumer Court, Sargodha (PLD 2019 Lahore 429), held that the Healthcare Commission is the only competent forum to investigate allegations of maladministration or malpractice by a healthcare service provider, and that the Consumer Court has no jurisdiction to adjudicate such matters. In Dr. Saiqa Yousaf v. The State (2024 P Cr. LJ 1852 Islamabad), Honorable Islamabad High Court held that where a special statutory mechanism exists to deal with medical negligence, such as the Healthcare Commission or PMDC, the same must be invoked first, and no criminal proceedings can be initiated without exhausting that remedy. The Court further held that determination of medical negligence requires expert opinion, and in the absence of such technical evidence, registration of FIR or continuation of criminal proceedings is without lawful justification.Thus, in light of the above-referred judgments, and keeping in view the overriding effect conferred upon the HCC Act under section 33, the answer to question No. 1 is that the Consumer Protection Court has no jurisdiction to entertain or decide complaints relating to alleged medical negligence in the presence of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission. The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Healthcare Commission (Complaints Management) Regulations, 2022 further obligate the Commission to entertain and inquire into complaints relating to malpractice, maladministration, quackery, unethical conduct, failure to follow medical standards, documentary irregularities, and violations affecting patient rights. The Regulations specifically define “malpractice” as improper, illegal, unethical, or unskilled professional conduct and empower the Commission to initiate inquiries, conduct inspections, seek expert opinions, and pass speaking orders in accordance with principles of natural justice. Therefore, failure to disclose a valid PMDC registration number, misrepresentation of qualifications, permitting unauthorized persons to practice medicine, or allowing healthcare establishments to function in violation of prescribed standards are all matters squarely falling within the jurisdiction and regulatory competence of the HCC.

case: W.P No. 530-M of 2025 Dr. Raqib Khan Vs Federation of Pakistan & others
remarks:
The preliminary issue before Court was whether the constitutional petition was maintainable despite the availability of a statutory appeal before the Medical Tribunal. The petitioner argued that section 6(2) of the Medical Tribunal Act, 2020 referred only to repealed enactments and did not expressly include orders passed under the Pakistan Medical and Dental Council Act, 2022.
The Court examined section 8 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 and the doctrine of legislative continuity, holding that where a statute is repealed and re-enacted, references to the repealed law in another enactment are ordinarily construed as references to the successor law unless a contrary intention appears.
The Court held that although the Pakistan Medical and Dental Council Act, 2022 was not specifically mentioned in section 6(2) of the Medical Tribunal Act, 2020, the appellate jurisdiction of the Medical Tribunal continued to cover decisions made under the successor legislation. Consequently, an adequate and efficacious alternate remedy of appeal was available to the petitioner. Therefore the constitutional petition was not maintainable due to the existence of the statutory remedy before the Medical Tribunal.
Where a statute referred to in a remedial provision is repealed and replaced by a successor enactment governing the same subject matter, the reference must ordinarily be construed as extending to the successor law by virtue of section 8 of the General Clauses Act, 1897. Accordingly, statutory appellate remedies remain available notwithstanding the repeal and re-enactment of the underlying legislation.

case: W.P No. 6436-P of 2026 Rafiq Khan and others Vs Federation of Pakistan through Federation Secretary and others
remarks: During the existence of an adequate alternate department remedy, a writ petition is ordinarily not maintainable. The cases where petitioners seek cancellation of Afghan Citizen Cards (ACC)/Proof of Registration (POR) Cards without first approaching the NADRA authorities and availing the prescribed department mechanism, such writ petitions shall not be maintainable before this Court.

case: W.P No. 401-P of 2026 with C.M No. 14-P of 2026 Muhammad Umair Vs Inspector General of Police, KP at Central Police Office (CPO), Peshawar & another.
remarks:
Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973—Article 199—The doctrine of Legitimate Expectation—— Held —The doctrine of legitimate expectation connotes that a person may have a reasonable expectation of being treated in a certain way by administrative authorities owing to some uniform practice or an explicit promise made by the concerned authority. In fact, a legitimate expectation ascends in consequence of a promise, assurance, practice or policy made, adopted or announced by or on behalf of government or a public authority. When such a legitimate expectation is obliterated, it affords a locus standi to challenge the administrative action and even, in the absence of a substantive right, a legitimate expectation may allow an individual to seek judicial review of a wrongdoing; and in deciding whether the expectation was legitimate or not, the Court may consider that the decision of the public authority has breached a legitimate expectation and, if it is proved, then the Court may annul the decision and direct the concerned authority/person to live up to the legitimate expectation. This doctrine is basically applied as a tool to watch over the actions of administrative authorities and in essence imposes obligations on all public authorities to act fair and square in all matters encompassing legitimate expectation.
The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Initial Appointment to Civil Posts (Relaxation of Upper Age Limit) Rules, 2008—Rule 3, 5, 6—Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation— Held— Once a candidate is allowed to participate in the entire selection process without any objection to his eligibility, the employer cannot, at a later stage, raise an objection regarding the same eligibility criteria that were either known or ought to have been known. The respondents’ conduct—permitting the petitioner to sit for the ETEA test, qualify it on merit, appear in the interview, secure recommendation by the Selection Committee, and ultimately receive a provisional offer of appointment—unequivocally indicates an implied acceptance of his entitlement to age relaxation as per the advertised terms and existing rules. This conduct created a legitimate expectation in the petitioner that he would be appointed, subject only to successful completion of the remaining formalities. The doctrine of legitimate expectation is an equitable principle evolved by the Courts for doing justice and there is no reason why it should be given only a limited application by way of defence.
Statutory Rules & Policy Decisions—Prospective vs. Retrospective application—Provincial Cabinet Decision dated 15.01.2025— Held— It is a fundamental principle of law that statutory rules, notifications, or policy decisions generally operate prospectively unless expressly stated otherwise or if their retrospective application is necessitated by clear legislative intent and is for public good, without adversely affecting vested rights. Applying a subsequent policy decision retrospectively to deny a right that had already accrued or was in the process of accrual would be unjust and unlawful. The ban imposed subsequent to the selection could not take away the rights of the petitioner which has already accrued to her and was also finalized. The Provincial Cabinet Decision of 15.01.2025, banning age relaxation, being prospective in nature, cannot be applied to the petitioner’s case, which commenced under the regime of the 2008 Rules and the terms of the advertisement.
Doctrine of Locus Poenitentiae—Administrative Orders— Held— Where an administrative order has taken effect and valuable rights have accrued therefrom, the authority’s power to withdraw or rescind such order becomes significantly restricted. The doctrine of locus poenitentiae cannot be invoked indefinitely to undo completed administrative actions, particularly where no fraud, concealment, misrepresentation, or misconduct is attributable to the beneficiary. Similarly, the petitioner cannot also be made to suffer for an omission or oversight on the part of public functionaries. The well-established maxim that “an act or omission of a public authority should prejudice none” squarely applies.
Ref: National Database and Registration Authority (NADRA) v. Jawad Khan (2023 SCMR 1381); Uzma Manzoor v. Vice-Chancellor, Khushal Khan Khattak University (2022 SCMR 694); Rukhsar Ali v. Government of N.W.F.P (2003 PLC 1453); Commissioner Inland Revenue and others v. Mekotex (Pvt.) Limited and others (PLD 2024 SC 1168); Fatima and others v. Government of Pakistan (2018 PLC 292); Muhammad Yasin v. Secretary, Government of Punjab (2008 PLC(CS) 303); Shahbaz v. Crown (PLD 1956 F.C. 46); Pakistan v. Muhammad Himayatullah (PLD 1969 SC 407).
Ref: Halsbury’s Laws of England, 3rd Edition Vol. II. (AIR 1965 S.C. 491). Petition Allowed.

case: W.P No. 448-M of 2026 Dilasa Khan & others Vs Shah Zaheer & others
remarks:
The Court held that the absence of settlement/revenue record does not automatically bar partition proceedings under Section 135 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Land Revenue Act, 1967. A Revenue Officer may assume jurisdiction if any one of the three conditions under Section 135 exists, namely: (i) recorded share in revenue record, (ii) a subsisting court decree determining the share, or (iii) a written acknowledgment of the right by interested parties.
The Court further held that a mere plea of title dispute does not oust the jurisdiction of Revenue Authorities. Only genuine and substantial disputes of title requiring adjudication by a Civil Court would prevent partition proceedings; otherwise, Revenue Authorities can proceed with partition.
Jurisdiction in partition matters depends on fulfillment of any condition under Section 135, not merely on the existence of settlement record. Case render by this court earlier in PLD 2022 Pesh. 147 also elucidate the same legal position.
